perturbation.original_text: “ Person ” shall be broadly interpreted to include, without limitation, any corporation, company, partnership, other entity or individual.
perturbation.changed_text: “ Person ” shall be narrowly interpreted to include only natural persons, specifically excluding any corporation, company, partnership, or other entity.
perturbation.explanation: The original definition of 'Person' includes various entities, while the modified definition restricts it to only natural persons. This can cause issues in contract enforcement, as the legal understanding of 'person' is broader.
perturbation.law_explanation: This modification contradicts the standard legal interpretation of 'person', which generally includes corporations, partnerships, and other entities, as outlined in the U.S. Code's rules of construction. By narrowly defining 'person', the contract creates ambiguity and potential unenforceability, since federal law dictates that 'person' extends beyond just individuals.

(1/4/1995) Back to Original Document << Previous TITLE 1 / CHAPTER 1 / § 1 Next >> [Print] [Print selection] [OLRC Home] Help 1 USC 1 : Words denoting number, gender, and so forth Text contains those laws in effect on July 12, 2025 From Title 1-GENERAL PROVISIONS CHAPTER 1-RULES OF CONSTRUCTION Jump To: Source Credit Miscellaneous Amendments Short Title §1. Words denoting number, gender, and so forth In determining the meaning of any Act of Congress, unless the context indicates otherwise- words importing the singular include and apply to several persons, parties, or things; words importing the plural include the singular; words importing the masculine gender include the feminine as well; words used in the present tense include the future as well as the present; the words "insane" and "insane person
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 1 U.S. Code § 1 - Words denoting number, gender, and so forth | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 1 U.S. Code § 1 - Words denoting number, gender, and so forth U.S. Code Notes prev | next In determining the meaning of any Act of Congress , unless the context indicates otherwise— words importing the singular include and apply to several persons , parties, or things; words importing the plural include the singular; words importing the masculine gender include the feminine as well; words used in the present tense include the future as well as the present; the words “ insane ” and “ insane person ” shall include every idiot, insane person , and person non compos mentis; the words “ person ” and “whoever” include corporations, companies, associations, firms, partnerships, societies, and joint stock companies, as well as individuals ; “ officer ” includes any person authorized by law to perform the duties of the office; “ signature ” or “ subscription ” includes a mark when the person making the same intended it as such; “ oath ” includes affirmation, and “ sworn ” includes affirmed; “ writing ” includes printing and typewriting and reproductions of visual symbols by photographing, multigraphing, mimeographing, manifolding, or otherwise. (July 30, 1947, ch. 388, 61 Stat. 633 ; June 25, 1948, ch. 645, § 6, 62 Stat. 859 ; Oct. 31, 1951, ch. 655, § 1, 65 Stat. 710 ; Pub. L. 112–231, § 2(a) , Dec. 28, 2012 , 126 Stat. 1619 .) Editorial Notes Amendments 2012— Pub. L. 112–231 , in fifth clause after opening clause, struck out “and ‘lunatic’ ” before “shall include every” and “lunatic,” before “insane person, ”. 1951—Act Oct. 31, 1951 , substituted, in fourth clause after opening clause, “used” for “use”. 1948—Act June 25, 1948 , included “tense”, “whoever”, “signature” , “subscription” , “writing” and a broader definition of “person” . Statutory Notes and Related Subs
perturbation.justification: The modified text directly contradicts the established legal definition of 'person' as outlined in 1 U.S. Code § 1. The original text includes various entities within the definition of 'Person,' whereas the modified text explicitly excludes corporations, companies, partnerships, or other entities, limiting the definition to only natural persons. 1 U.S. Code § 1 clearly states that the words “ person ” and “whoever” include corporations, companies, associations, firms, partnerships, societies, and joint stock companies, as well as individuals. This direct contradiction makes the modified clause likely to fail legal scrutiny.

perturbation.original_text: Confidential Information " shall mean all confidential or proprietary written, recorded, electronic or oral information or data (including without limitation research, developmental, engineering, manufacturing, technical, marketing, sales, financial, operating, performance, cost, business and process information or data, trade secrets, discoveries, ideas, designs, data, source code, object code, processes, computer programs, developments, flow diagrams, know-how, and computer programming and other software and software techniques) provided (whether such confidentiality or proprietary status is indicated orally or in writing, whether or not the specific words "confidential" or "proprietary" are used) to a Party (the “Receiving Party”) by the other Party (the “Disclosing Party”) in the course of the exchange of such information or data between the Parties.
perturbation.changed_text: Confidential Information " shall mean any information that the Disclosing Party marks as 'Confidential' in a conspicuous manner at the time of disclosure. Information not explicitly marked as such is not considered Confidential Information, regardless of its nature or sensitivity.
perturbation.explanation: The original definition is broad, while the modified definition requires explicit marking as 'Confidential'. This weakens the protection of trade secrets and other sensitive information.
perturbation.law_explanation: This modification contradicts the Uniform Trade Secrets Act (UTSA), which defines a trade secret without requiring explicit marking. The UTSA protects information that derives independent economic value from not being generally known, and is subject to reasonable efforts to maintain its secrecy. By requiring explicit marking, the modified contract fails to protect trade secrets under the standard established by the UTSA.

perturbation.justification: The modification introduces a Misaligned Terminology Legal Contradiction. The original text provides a broad definition of 'Confidential Information,' while the changed text narrows it, requiring explicit marking. This contradicts the Uniform Trade Secrets Act (UTSA), as indicated in the Law Explanation, which protects trade secrets based on their economic value and reasonable efforts to maintain secrecy, not solely on explicit marking. Scraped Law Snippet 2 mentions that the UTSA "provides states with a legal framework for improved trade secret protection to industry." By requiring explicit marking, the modified contract may fail to protect information that qualifies as a trade secret under UTSA standards but isn't explicitly marked as confidential, creating a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: The Receiving Party shall hold confidential and not disclose to any Person, without the prior written consent of the Disclosing Party, all Confidential Information and any information about the Proposed Transaction, or the terms or conditions or any other facts relating thereto, including, without limitation, the fact that discussions are taking place with respect thereto or the status thereof, or the fact that Confidential Information has been made available to the Receiving Party or its Representatives
perturbation.changed_text: The Receiving Party shall endeavor to hold confidential and not disclose to any Person, without the prior written consent of the Disclosing Party, all Confidential Information and any information about the Proposed Transaction, or the terms or conditions or any other facts relating thereto, including, without limitation, the fact that discussions are taking place with respect thereto or the status thereof, or the fact that Confidential Information has been made available to the Receiving Party or its Representatives
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'shall hold confidential' to 'shall endeavor to hold confidential' weakens the obligation and introduces ambiguity. The original language creates a clear duty, the changed language suggests an attempt, but not necessarily an obligation to do so.
perturbation.law_explanation: This modification contradicts the implied duty of good faith and fair dealing in contract law, as outlined in the Restatement (Second) of Contracts § 205. By weakening the obligation to hold information confidential, the modification introduces ambiguity and could be interpreted as a lack of commitment to the core purpose of the agreement, thus violating the implied duty of good faith.

perturbation.justification: The original contract language 'shall hold confidential' creates a clear and enforceable obligation. The changed language, 'shall endeavor to hold confidential,' weakens this obligation, making it ambiguous and potentially unenforceable. The Perturbation Explanation confirms that this introduces ambiguity and suggests an attempt rather than an obligation. Although there aren't any helpful scraped law snippets, the change directly undermines the intended purpose of the confidentiality clause, creating a significant legal contradiction, as it introduces uncertainty about whether the Receiving Party is truly bound to maintain confidentiality. This falls under the Misaligned Terminology - Legal Contradiction definition.

perturbation.original_text: The Receiving Party shall cause all its Representatives to observe the terms of this Agreement and shall be responsible for any breach of the terms of this Agreement by it or its Representatives
perturbation.changed_text: The Receiving Party shall encourage its Representatives to observe the terms of this Agreement and shall only be responsible for breaches directly caused by the Receiving Party's intentional actions.
perturbation.explanation: The original language makes the Receiving Party responsible for any breach by its Representatives. The modified language limits responsibility to only intentional actions of the Receiving Party.
perturbation.law_explanation: This modification contradicts the principle of respondeat superior in agency law, as reflected in the Restatement (Third) of Agency § 7.07. This legal doctrine holds employers or principals liable for the torts of employees or agents committed within the scope of their employment or agency. By limiting the Receiving Party's responsibility to only intentional actions, the modified contract attempts to circumvent the legal principle of vicarious liability.

perturbation.justification: The original contract holds the Receiving Party responsible for *any* breach by its Representatives, while the changed text limits this responsibility to breaches *directly caused by the Receiving Party's intentional actions*. The LLM explanation indicates this contradicts the principle of respondeat superior, which holds principals liable for the actions of their agents. While Scraped Law Snippets are not useful in this example, the Perturbation Explanation clarifies the shift in responsibility, and the Law Explanation provides the legal basis for the contradiction. This is a direct and impactful change that weakens the original obligation. Therefore, the modified clause would likely fail legal scrutiny.

perturbation.original_text: Unless sooner terminated by mutual written Agreement of the Parties hereto, this Agreement and the obligations hereunder shall terminate one (1) year from the date hereof.
perturbation.changed_text: Unless sooner terminated by mutual written Agreement of the Parties hereto, this Agreement and the obligations hereunder shall terminate one (1) year from the date hereof, except that obligations related to trade secrets shall persist only if the Disclosing Party sends a renewal notice every six months.
perturbation.explanation: Adding a six-month renewal requirement for trade secret obligations introduces an arbitrary condition that isn't aligned with standard trade secret protection, potentially weakening it.
perturbation.law_explanation: This modification introduces a condition (renewal notice) for trade secret protection that is not found in the Uniform Trade Secrets Act (UTSA). Under the UTSA, trade secret protection lasts as long as the information remains a trade secret and the owner takes reasonable measures to protect its secrecy. By adding a renewal requirement, the contract creates a scenario where trade secrets could lose protection even if they still meet the UTSA's criteria.
perturbation.justification: The modification introduces a renewal requirement for trade secret protection, which is inconsistent with the standard legal understanding where protection lasts as long as the information remains a trade secret and the owner takes reasonable measures to protect its secrecy as suggested by the LLM. The changed text creates a scenario where trade secrets could lose protection even if they still meet the criteria for trade secret protection based on established legal norms. The misaligned terminology, specifically the introduction of the renewal notice, creates a contradiction.
